note_data.title: Limited company accounts
keyPoints: **Share capital** represents the nominal value of shares issued to shareholders and forms part of equity in the statement of financial position
**Ordinary shares** carry voting rights and variable dividends, while **preference shares** receive fixed dividends before ordinary shareholders
**Debentures** are long-term loans to the company that must be repaid and carry fixed interest rates shown as finance costs
**Retained earnings** are accumulated profits kept in the business and appear in equity section of the statement of financial position
**Dividends** are distributions of profit to shareholders and reduce retained earnings when declared
**Share premium** is the excess amount paid above nominal value when shares are issued at a premium
**Authorised share capital** is the maximum value of shares that can be issued as stated in the memorandum of association
**Issued share capital** is the actual nominal value of shares that have been sold to shareholders
Debenture interest is an **expense** charged to profit or loss account, while dividends are an **appropriation** of profit

definitions.definition: A business organization that has separate legal entity from its owners with limited liability for shareholders
The nominal value of shares issued by a company to its shareholders
A long-term loan to a company with fixed interest rate and specified repayment date
Accumulated profits that have been kept in the business rather than distributed as dividends
A distribution of company profits to shareholders in proportion to their shareholding

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Show working clearly and label your final answer
Use correct format with proper headings and classifications

modelAnswer.question: Explain the difference between ordinary shares and preference shares. (4 marks)
modelAnswer.answer: Ordinary shares carry voting rights at company meetings while preference shares usually have no voting rights. Ordinary shareholders receive variable dividends that depend on company profits while preference shareholders receive fixed dividends. Preference shareholders have priority over ordinary shareholders when dividends are distributed. In the event of liquidation, preference shareholders are paid before ordinary shareholders but after creditors.

commonMistakes: Never say debenture interest is an appropriation of profit — it is an expense in profit or loss account
Students confuse authorised and issued share capital — authorised is maximum allowed, issued is actual amount sold
Never include dividends proposed but not declared in current year's profit and loss appropriation account
Students forget that share premium cannot be used to pay dividends — only retained earnings can be distributed
Never show debentures as part of equity — they are non-current liabilities
examinerTips: Limited company accounts appear in every Paper 2 - focus on statement of financial position format
Always distinguish between expense (debenture interest) and appropriation (dividends) in extended questions
Remember the order: preference dividends paid before ordinary dividends
Learn the equity section layout: share capital + share premium + retained earnings
formulas: Retained earnings = Opening retained earnings + Net profit - Dividends paid
Earnings per share = Net profit after tax ÷ Number of ordinary shares
Dividend per share = Total dividends ÷ Number of shares
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Statement of Financial Position equity section showing: Share capital (ordinary + preference), Share premium, Retained earnings in vertical format with proper classifications and totals
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2023 P2 Q4 (12 marks) — they asked students to prepare the trading and profit & loss account and balance sheet for XYZ Ltd from a trial balance, including adjustments for depreciation and provisions
O/N 2022 P2 Q3 (8 marks) — required calculation of gross profit margin and net profit margin for ABC Limited, with comparison to previous year and comment on performance
M/J 2022 P2 Q5 (10 marks) — students had to show appropriation of profit including corporation tax, dividends proposed, and transfer to general reserves for a public limited company
O/N 2021 P2 Q2 (6 marks) — tested distinction between authorized and issued share capital, and calculation of shareholders' equity from given balance sheet extract

workedExample.question: DEF Limited has the following information for the year ended 31 December 2023: Net profit before tax $45,000; Corporation tax $9,000; Interim dividend paid $6,000; Final dividend proposed $8,000; Retained earnings brought forward $25,000. Prepare the appropriation account. (6 marks)
steps: Step 1: Start with net profit before tax of $45,000
Step 2: Deduct corporation tax provision of $9,000 to get profit after tax
Step 3: Add retained earnings brought forward of $25,000
Step 4: Deduct total dividends: interim paid $6,000 + final proposed $8,000 = $14,000
Step 5: Calculate retained earnings carried forward
workedExample.answer:
DEF Limited - Appropriation Account for year ended 31 Dec 2023
Net profit before tax: $45,000
Less: Corporation tax: ($9,000)
Profit after tax: $36,000
Add: Retained earnings b/f: $25,000
Available for appropriation: $61,000
Less: Interim dividend paid: ($6,000)
Less: Final dividend proposed: ($8,000)
Retained earnings c/f: $47,000
workedExample.markScheme: 1 mark each for: correct format/headings, net profit before tax figure, corporation tax deduction, adding retained earnings b/f, both dividend deductions, final retained earnings calculation

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates confused authorized capital with issued capital' — always clearly distinguish between the maximum capital a company can issue versus what has actually been issued to shareholders

Examiner Report 2022: 'Students frequently omitted corporation tax from the appropriation account' — remember that limited companies must provide for corporation tax on profits, unlike sole traders and partnerships

Examiner Report 2021: 'Common error was treating proposed dividends as paid dividends' — proposed dividends are a current liability in the balance sheet, while paid dividends go through the appropriation account as cash outflow
note_data.keyDiagram: Students must be able to draw the structure of a limited company balance sheet showing: 1) Fixed Assets section (cost, accumulated depreciation, net book value), 2) Current Assets (stock, debtors, bank, cash), 3) Current Liabilities (creditors, proposed dividends, accruals), 4) Net Current Assets calculation, 5) Long-term Liabilities, 6) Net Assets total, 7) Financed by section showing Share Capital (authorized vs issued), Reserves (capital reserves, revenue reserves including retained earnings), and total Shareholders' Equity. The diagram should clearly show the dual aspect where Net Assets = Shareholders' Equity.
note_data.quickRecap: Limited companies have separate legal existence and limited liability for shareholders. Their accounts include corporation tax provisions and dividend appropriations. Balance sheets show authorized vs issued share capital, and distinguish between capital reserves (non-distributable) and revenue reserves (distributable as dividends). Key ratios include gross profit margin, net profit margin, and return on capital employed for performance analysis.
